Hey, welcome back and welcome to the module on looping, which is the strategy or more accurately the secret sauce I created for the back half of the straight line system. Essentially what looping allows you to do is take any prospect who's sitting on the fence, meaning after you've asked for the order for the first time, they've now come back to you with one of the common objections like let me think about it, let me call you back, bedtime a year, talk to my wife, and you address that objection in a way that does not break for a poor, I repeat, does not break for a poor, and then it's caused you to blow the sail and you continue now moving the prospect down the straight line and ultimately close the deal. Just so you know, the reason I emphasize those four words does not break for a poor is because that's what typically happens during the so-called objection handling phase of the sale. The average salesperson tends to break rapport with their prospect and ends up blowing the sail and result of that. In fact, I see this happen a thousand times. A salesman breezes through the front half of the sale, everything is going fine. He's in complete control, he's in tight rapport with his prospect, he gave an awesome presentation, the prospect is fully engaged, he's loved the product. See, it looks like one of those amazing lay down sales where there's basically an objection free close and then, out of nowhere, bam, reality hits. Like virtually every prospect who came before this one, he hits you with a common objection. Now which particular objection hits you with is completely irrelevant because in a matter of moments that spirit of collaboration that's very typical in the front half of the sale has now devolved into an outright confrontation in the back half of the sale as the salesperson tries to convince his prospect that his objection basically wasn't called for. In other words, no matter how you slice it, what the salesman is saying to the prospect right now is, my thinking is right and your thinking is wrong, which is basically the worst thing you could say to someone if you want to stay in rapport with them. Yet that's exactly what I see happen all over the world. And in a matter of seconds, what looked to be a potential lay down or at least let's say a 50-50 shot of closing has now turned into a complete and utter disaster. The salesman has broken rapport, the prospect is now shifting objections, the sales spiral out of control and you can trace every last bit of this back to that first toxic moment where the salesperson tried to overcome the prospect objection and shattered their rapport as a result of that. And herein lies the true beauty of looping. You see what looping allows you to do is take any one of the common objections and use it as an opportunity to increase your prospect's level of certainty for each of the three tens and if necessary lower their action threshold or add on pain and then seamlessly transition into a close and you do all of that without breaking rapport. And then if you get hit with another objection, you're simply going to repeat that process again and again and again in response to each new objection. Now in straight line parlance, we refer to each new repetition, each repeat, we call it a loop and we refer to the process itself as looping or running a loop. Now real quick here, let me just show you a graphic of the straight line. I want to show you the exact spot where you are right now when the process of looping begins which is actually right here after you've asked for the order for the first time. And just to be clear here, when you ask for the order for the first time, you did it in a calm, sincere tone using a simple yet extremely effective language pattern from the ones I gave you in the scripting module. For example, you could say, and believe me Bill, if you do even half as well as the rest of my clients in this program, you're going to be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough? And of course those last three words were said in the reasonable manton. Sound fair enough? Like sound fair enough? Or sound fair enough? You get it? Go ahead and make my day. Sound fair enough? You want to do it in a very calm, cool and collected tone, the reasonable man. It's essentially like you almost want to be so smooth as silk. So the point where your tonality is so calm and so reasonable that only a complete mystery with no social graces will be rude enough to say no to you. And by the way, just so you know, that's exactly what your goal is here. You want to make it so easy and so low pressure for your prospect that you feel almost compelled to cave into you right on the spot and give you the order the first time you ask for it. And it would be absolutely fabulous if sales actually worked that way. Meaning that by perfectly extruding the front half of the sale, you could eliminate the need for the back half of the sale completely, right? I mean, that would be totally awesome. But unfortunately, sales does not work that way. No matter how great you sound, no matter how on target your words are, there's still a 95 plus percent chance that your prospect is going to hit you with some type of common objection. Even if he's forced to conjure one up that's completely bogus. See, it's very, very rare that a prospect will come straight out and tell you what's truly holding them back, right? And of course, we went through this in the earlier modules. It's either a lack of certainty that's holding them back for one, two, or even all three of the three tens, or perhaps they have a very high action threshold, or maybe they're in complete denial of their pain. And the danger here is that this is the spot in the sales encounter where a typical sales person ends up plunging into the so-called sales death spiral. Let me explain what that is. It starts when the prospect hits you with a bogus objection to which you, the salesperson, spit back a canned response designed to overcome that objection. And you ask for the order again. But think this through for a second. Since the objection they gave you was bogus, you now just answered their question and they didn't even care about your answer. So what do they do? They simply hop to a new objection, right? And then what do you do? You answer that objection. And they're like, okay, well, that was, didn't really matter. I didn't care about it. So they hop to another objection. And on and on this death spiral goes where they hit you with a bogus objection and you answer it, another bogus objection. It's a complete waste of time and you're basically done. Remember, objections are merely stalls or more specifically smoke screens for what? Uncertainty and what looping allows you to do is quickly and elegantly get past these smoke screens so you can seamlessly transition to the heart of what truly holds them back, which is this lack of certainty. So in short, looping shows you how to use each of the common objections as its own opportunity to build absolute certainty for each of the three tens until you've gotten your prospect to a point of such absolute certainty that their collective level of certainty for all three tens exceeds their action threshold, at which point, bam, everyone starts to buy. That's the process of looping. And I promise you it is going to be a total game changer for you. And with that, it's now time to dive into the main body of this module. I don't have a quiz for you right now. So let's just rock on and I'll see you in the next lesson.
